stem: An adult client has been diagnosed with stroke and right-sided paralysis. Which sign is most important for the nurse to post in the client’s room?
rationale: If the arm is utilized for lifting, the shoulder might dislocate easily since the paralyzed muscles are unable to provide resistance. The sign will help avoid this common injury in paralyzed patients by alerting healthcare providers to it. The client is difficulty hearing
The client is paralyzed on the right side

stem: After a major power outage, a confused client with an unsteady gait arrives at an emergency unit. Which action should the nurse take first?
rationale: The client is in danger of falling due to her uneven walk. Just before starting the exam, the nurse should make sure the client is safe. stem: A client has been diagnosed with disseminated herpes zoster. Which observation by the nurse would require intervention?

stem: A nurse observed that another staff entered the client's room while wearing gloves, gown, and mask. The nurse anticipates that the staff is most likely to be caring for which of the following clients?

stem: A nurse is teaching a client how to use incentive spirometry. Which instruction should the nurse include in the teaching?

stem: A home health nurse is assessing the home environment of a client newly diagnosed with legal blindness. Which instruction should the nurse give to this client to ensure safety at home?
rationale: Burns from hot water is less likely if the hot water tank's thermostat is set at 100 degrees. To guarantee the client's safety, the nurse should start by advising this. Burns from hot water is less likely if the hot water tank's thermostat is set at 100 degrees. To guarantee the client's safety, the nurse should start by advising this. It is possible to teach blind clients how to operate household electrical items securely. The nurse shouldn't prescribe this as their first course of action. Use battery-operated appliances and not the electrical appliances

stem: A hospital has activated disaster emergency and expects to receive victims in less than an hour. The team leader is informed about discharging one of the following clients to make necessary emergency space for victims. Which client should the nurse plan to discharge?
rationale: The most reliable client is this one. Clients who have had a total hip replacement are normally sent home or to a rehabilitation center on the second or third postoperative day. stem: A nurse provides teaching to the clients regarding skateboard safety. Which of the following instructions by the nurse is most appropriate?

From a developmental standpoint, they struggle to defend themselves against harm. Skateboards are made for a variety of purposes (slalom, freestyle, or speed). stem: A nurse has administered proparacaine hydrochloride before each eye examination of a client with an eye injury due to an acid splash. Which action should the nurse take next?
rationale: When the eye is anesthetized, rubbing or touching it may harm the cornea. The primary concern is to make sure the client is safe by telling them not to touch their eyes. Topical anesthesia is provided by proparacaine hydrochloride. Glaucoma may be detected using intraocular pressure.

Measure intraocular pressure of the client
Ask the client to avoid touching the eye

stem: An elderly client has been diagnosed with decreased hearing equity. The family is concerned because the client insists on going for evening walks alone. What action by the nurse is most appropriate?
rationale: Optimizing health and wellbeing in order to maintain autonomy and independence is one of the older adult client's main objectives. The best advice is to see an audiologist since they may provide hearing aids that would improve the client's freedom and autonomy.

Ask the client to visit an audiologist

stem: An elderly client who is agitated is diagnosed with a urinary tract infection and tries to get out of bed. The healthcare provider has ordered the application of vest restraints. Which action should the nurse take?
rationale: In general, restraining devices should be removed at least every two hours to undertake skin assessments and preserve a range of motion. Institutional rules should be observed in this regard. Never attach a restraint to the side rails of a bed frame. Wrist restraints may cause the client to get more agitated than a vest restraint. Thus, the nurse should instead use a quick-release knot to connect it to a sturdy part of the bed (or chair) to allow for position adjustments. It is best to use the strategy with the fewest restrictions. The client should be informed by the nurse that a restraining device is being used to protect the client. The client, however, is unable to provide informed consent due to their changed mental state. In this case, the client's proxy must provide it.
options: Secure the restraints to the side rails of the bed
Ask the healthcare provider to replace the prescription with wrist restraints
Obtain the client’s permission to apply restraints
Remove restraints every 2 hours to assess the client

stem: A client has been diagnosed with suspected meningococcal meningitis. Which action should the nurse take first?
rationale: Isolation measures, the commencement of antibiotic medication, and maintaining optimal hydration are all part of the first therapeutic care of acute bacterial meningitis. Nurses should take the appropriate steps to keep themselves and others safe from infection.
options: Apply seizure protocol
Assess neurological status
Keep the client in droplet isolation
Check vital signs

stem: A nurse is planning care for four clients. Which of the following clients require airborne isolation?
rationale: The client who requires airborne precautions has a positive PPD (purified protein derivative) and a positive x-ray for a suspicious tuberculin lesion.
options: A client with advanced lung carcinoma
A client with viral pneumonia

stem: A nurse is teaching infection control measures to a group of nursing students. Which intervention is the most important in preventing infections during patient care?
rationale: Handwashing is still the most efficient approach to prevent the transmission of illness. Too often, however, nurses do not employ basic handwashing practices and do not instruct their patients to do so. Before and after handling the client, as well as before entering the nursing bag, nurses must wash their hands.
options: Wearing gloves
Hand hygiene

stem: During the pre-employment screening, a person was diagnosed with tuberculosis. He was really frighted about spreading it to others, especially his family. The nurse provides instructions about common ways of tubercle bacillus transmission to others. Which of the following contamination causes this transmission?
rationale: The common cold is mostly transmitted by the hands. Droplet nuclei are the most common mode of transmission for the tubercle bacillus. The bacillus is present in the air as a result of an infected person's coughing, sneezing, and sputum expectoration. The tubercle bacillus is not spread by contaminated food. Contact with contaminated food or water might result in salmonella, infectious hepatitis, typhoid, or cholera epidemics. The tubercle bacillus is not spread by dining utensils. Some foreign bacteria can spread through reservoirs like linens or dining utensils.
options: Hands
Eating in the same utensils
Taking milk products
Droplet nuclei

stem: A 2-year-old client has been diagnosed with febrile seizures. Which of the following nursing actions would be the priority for this client?
rationale: Preparing for routine laboratory tests is not as important as avoiding harm and improving safety. Preparing for routine laboratory tests is not as important as avoiding harm and improving safety. A cooling blanket must be authorized by a physician and is normally not used unless other techniques of fever control have failed. The youngster is suffering from febrile seizures. options: Get a stat CBC done
Place urine specimen bottle at bedside
Place a cooling mattress on the bed
Pad the side rails

stem: A nurse is providing teaching to a client with major burns affecting 25% of the body regarding her wound management at home. Which of the following client’s statements would most satisfy the nurse as a sign of effective teaching?
rationale: Bathing or bathing as normal is permissible, with the use of a light detergent soap such as Ivory Snow. This cleans the wounds, especially the open ones, and eliminates dead tissue. The client is instructed to record changes in wound healing, such as the emergence of blisters, symptoms of infection, and the reopening of a previously healed region. Sterile dressings are used until the wound is examined and a treatment plan is devised. The Jobs garment is intended to apply continual pressure to the newly formed healthy tissue in order to encourage adhesion to the underlying structure and avoid hypertrophic scarring. The garment must be worn for 23 hours each day to be effective. It is removed to allow for wound inspection and treatment, as well as washing. The client should be aware that wound infection is possible; any indicators of infection, such as fever, redness, discomfort, warmth in and around the wound, and increased or foul-smelling discharge, should be reported promptly.
options: “I will take sponge baths at home to avoid my wounds getting exposed to excessive water.”
“In case of my healed area cracks up, I will cover it with a sterile dressing and then report to the physician.”
“I will remove my jobst elastic garment only when I am going to bed.”
“Low-grade fever can occur, so I can take Tylenol every 4 hours.”

stem: A postop client appears confused and agitated and tries to climb over the side rails. Which action should the nurse take most appropriately to calm this client?
rationale: Staff must visit the client on a regular basis to orient him and check his safety. Phone calls from his family will not assist a client who is attempting to climb over the side railings and may even endanger him. Placing the client in a bright, bustling environment would very certainly contribute to his bewilderment. The environment has a vital role in injury prevention. Talking gently and using soothing music might help to soothe an upset client.
options: Limit visitors to see the client
Allow family’s phone calls

Provide quiet music and speak in a soothing voice

stem: A nurse is providing teaching to a client who is about to have cervical radiation due to cancer. Which statement by the client would indicate that the teaching is effective?
rationale: The client will be on a clear liquid diet or one with very little residue. Burgers and french fries are not permitted. Pregnant women should avoid close contact with someone undergoing internal radiation therapy. The radiation may cause harm to the fetus. This assertion is false. The client is no longer polluted with radioactivity as soon as the radiation source is withdrawn (usually 36 to 72 hours after insertion). Craft projects frequently necessitate client sitting. During the time the rods are in place, the client must stay flat with very minimal head elevation.
options: She asks her husband to bring McDonald's mc chicken burger and fries as she hates hospital food
She calls her pregnant daughter and tells her to visit her once she reaches back home

stem: A nurse in charge is performing an infection control audit of the unit. Which of the following observations would require the nurse in charge to educate the staff?
rationale: When feeding a client, there is no need to wear gloves. In all instances, however, universal precautions (handling all blood and bodily fluids as though they are infectious) should be followed. Active TB patients should be given respiratory precautions. It is acceptable to have the client wear a mask before exiting his private room. Persons suffering from exudative lesions or weeping dermatitis should refrain from providing direct client care or using client-care equipment until the disease has resolved. stem: A nurse instructor supervises an enrolled nurse in sterile wound dressing. After hand hygiene, the nurse wears sterile gloves and removes the old dressing. She then discards the old pair of gloves and wears a new pair of sterile gloves to clean and redress the wound. Which action by the charge nurse is most appropriate?
rationale: To remove the old dressing, nonsterile gloves are sufficient. However, the usage of sterile gloves does not endanger the client. Therefore, this may be discussed later. The staff nurse is making two mistakes. To remove the old dressing, nonsterile gloves are sufficient. After removing the contaminated dressing and before donning sterile gloves to clean and treat the wound, the nurse should wash her hands. After removing the contaminated dressing and before donning the sterile gloves to clean and treat the wound, the nurse should wash her hands. Failure to do so jeopardizes client safety and should be brought to the nurse's notice right away. The staff nurse is making two mistakes. To remove the old dressing, nonsterile gloves are sufficient. However, the usage of sterile gloves does not endanger the client. Therefore, this may be discussed later. However, after removing the contaminated dressing and before donning sterile gloves to clean and treat the wound, the nurse should wash her hands. Failure to do so jeopardizes client safety and should be brought to the nurse's notice right away.
options: Tell the nurse that sterile gloves are not required for removing old dressing
Appreciate the nurse for applying good practice
Discuss the wound dressing technique on a later day
Tell the nurse that she should wash her hands after removing the old dressing

stem: A nurse is assessing the risk of falls for an elderly client at home. Which of the following should the nurse consider in this regard?
rationale: The setting should be well-lit to prevent falls. If required, night lighting should be employed. Other considerations include eliminating loose scatter rugs, cleaning up spills, and adding handrails and grab bars as needed. Regular exercise is not the most crucial element to consider. It is merely tangentially linked. The resting pulse rate has no bearing on fall prevention. The consumption of salt has no direct relationship with the prevention of falls.
options: Client’s salt intake
Client’s heart rate at rest
Client’s regular exercise level

stem: A nurse is providing teaching to a client regarding wound care that he needs to perform himself at home. Which statement by the client indicates that he has appropriately understood the importance of asepsis during wound care?
rationale: Anything that has been dropped on the floor has lost its sterility and should not be utilized. The statement demonstrates a lack of comprehension. Anything that has been dropped on the floor has lost its sterility and should not be utilized. The statement demonstrates a lack of comprehension. If there is any uncertainty regarding an instrument's or dressing's sterility, it should not be utilized. Before putting on the sterile gloves, soak the 4 X 4s. options: “I will reuse the 4x4 gauze if it fell on the floor unless it's apparently clean.”

“If I doubt anything’s sterility, I won't use it.”
“I will open the bottle of sterile normal saline to soak 4x4 gauze while wearing sterile gloves.”

stem: A nurse is assisting a CNA regarding caring for a client on blood body fluid isolation. Which action by the CNA would indicate that the teaching is effective?

Gloves should be used whenever there is contact with blood or bodily fluids, nonintact skin or mucous membranes, handling filthy things, or performing venipuncture. Gowns should be worn during procedures that are likely to result in blood or bodily fluid spills. If the client's hygiene is inadequate, a private room is advised.
options: CNA wears a mask to contact all the clients

stem: A nurse asks a group of new nurses to pick the correct action by the following people in preventing HIV transmission. Which of these should the nurses pick as a correct answer?
rationale: Needles that have drawn blood should not be recapped. If recapping is required, a hemostat or similar tool should be utilized. Never, ever use your hand. Because there is no touch with mucous membranes while feeding, gloves are not required. Although HIV can be found in minute levels in saliva, the virus cannot enter the body through unbroken skin. The query has no indication of damaged skin. Masks and protective eyewear are recommended where there is a high risk of splashing blood-contaminated bodily fluids. Suctioning a tracheostomy nearly always causes coughing, which may produce droplets that spray the health care provider. Clients who are regularly suctioned or who have undergone invasive surgery, such as a tracheostomy, are more likely to have blood in their sputum. There is no reason to prevent pregnant personnel from caring for AIDS patients if universal safeguards are taken.

stem: A child is admitted with a Staph skin infection. His brother also develops this infection. The nurse suspects that which of the following behavior of the children is causing the infection transmission?

stem: A home health nurse evaluates the client’s level of understanding of preventing AIDS transmission. Which of the following client’s statements indicates the highest level of understanding?
rationale: The AIDS virus spreads through sexual interaction and direct contact with bodily fluids such as blood. Casual contact with other persons does not increase the risk of AIDS transmission. There is no need for the client to miss his college classes unless he is really unwell. Touch sports are not prohibited unless there is a high risk of bleeding and direct contact with others. Casual contact with other persons does not increase the risk of AIDS transmission. There is no reason to avoid having casual contact with kids.
