On examination, his gait is ataxic and fundoscopy shows optic disc edema. The nurse will notify the physician about which of the following complications in this client?
rationale: Raised intracranial hemorrhage is a complication of encephalitis and is shown clinically by ataxic gait, edema of the optic disc, blurred vision, double vision, headache, altered level of consciousness and seizures.
options: Cerebral edema
Raised intracranial pressure
Intraparenchymal hemorrhage
Subarachnoid hemorrhage
Hydrocephalus

stem: Out of the following nursing interventions which one are indicated and which ones are contraindicated in this case? Mark them on the table below.
rationale: See rationale.
rows: Keep the head of the bed elevated to more than 30 degrees
Encourage rebreathing into a paper bag

stem: On the table below mark which parameters would indicate whether the client’s condition is improving, unchanged or worsening.
rationale: See rationale.

stem: A client with encephalitis is being discharged from the hospital after achieving adequate recovery. In the list below, highlight the advice the nurse will provide him with for self-care.
rationale: Self-care teaching to clients recovering from encephalitis includes the following information: Wear full sleeved shirts and long pants when outside at dusk or early morning Wear a mosquito repellent with 10-30% DEET Take acetaminophen for fever Do not discontinue medicine abruptly Increase fluid intake Look for signs of viral infection such as birds dying Get at least 8 hours of sleep Get plenty of rest Category: Neurology, infectious diseases, multiple response question
tokens: Wear full sleeved shirts and long pants when outside at dusk or early morning
Wear a mosquito repellent with 10-30% DEET
There should not be any major physical activity before meals
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: A 2-year-old male is being discharged after recovering from Reye’s Syndrome. Out of the following statements which one is appropriate advice for future prevention of this disease?
rationale: While there are many causes of Reye’s syndrome the most well known cause is the use of aspirin during a viral infection. Category: Neurology, client self-care, multiple choice question Questions 90 to 95 are based on the case study given below Category: Neurology, immunology, case study A 25-year-old male is brought to the Emergency Department with the complaint of progressive weakness of both legs for the past week and inability to walk since this morning due to muscle weakness. His past medical history shows that he had gastroenteritis two weeks ago and a stool sample showed the presence of c. jejuni. He was also complaining of tingling and paresthesias in the fingertips of both hands. On examination his vital signs are as follows: Pulse 86 bpm Respiratory rate 18 bpm Temperature 98.6 F Blood pressure 130 O2 Saturation: 97% A nerve conduction study was done which showed absent nerve conduction in the lower limbs.
options: Do not give Paracetamol during an infection
Do not give antibiotics during a throat infection
Do not give aspirin for a fever
Do not nebulize the child for upper respiratory tract symptoms
Do not give any medication for fever until approved by a doctor

stem: Out of the diagnoses listed below, which one is most likely in this scenario?
rationale: The case scenario shows a typical case of Guillain-barre syndrome which occurs a few weeks after a gastrointestinal infection and starts with progressive weakness of the lower limbs and gradually involving the upper limbs.
options: Multiple sclerosis
Poliomyelitis
Guillain-barre syndrome
Transverse myelitis
Stroke

stem: Which cues in the client’s history, examination and laboratory findings support a diagnosis of Guillain-barre syndrome? Mark them on the list given below.
rationale: There is no gender and age predilection in Guillain-barre syndrome. Progressive weakness with sudden paralysis are typical findings of GBS. A history of a gastrointestinal infection, especially with campylobacter jejuni are also associated with development of GBS. Since there is dysfunction of the sensory nerves patients may also complain of tingling and paresthesias. Loss of nerve conduction on electrophysiologic study is a typical finding in GBS.
options: 25-year-old
Male
Progressive weakness of both legs for the past week
Inability to walk since this morning due to muscle weakness
History of gastroenteritis two weeks ago
C. jejuni infection
Tingling and paresthesias in the fingertips of both hands.
Pulse 86 bpm
Respiratory rate 18 bpm
Temperature 98.6 F
Blood pressure 130
O2 Saturation: 97%
Absent nerve conduction in the lower limbs.

stem: Out of the nursing interventions listed below which ones are indicated and contraindicated in this client?
rationale: See rationale.
rows: Maintain nil per oral status to prevent aspiration
Call client’s caretaker for decision making

stem: On the second day of admission, the client started complaining of weakness in both upper limbs and on examination, power was 3/5. She also started experiencing slurring of speech and had difficulty swallowing any food or water. The client is at risk of ________________ as evidenced by _______________________.
rationale: The progression of weakness to the upper limbs shows that the disease process is still progressing proximally. This, combined with the cranial nerve involvement (evidenced by slurring and dysphagia) places the patient at high risk of respiratory compromise as the respiratory muscles will most likely also be affected.
options: Stroke; slurring and dysphagia
Respiratory compromise; slurring, dysphagia and upper limb weakness
Permanent paralysis; upper limb weakness
Developing seizures; upper limb weakness
Developing a neuropathy; slurring, dysphagia and upper limb weakness

stem: Out of the client parameters listed below, which ones show that the client’s condition is improving, unchanged or worsening. Mark them on the table below.
rationale: See rationale.

stem: The input-output charting of the client shows the following trend.
rationale: Urinary retention may occur in Guillain-barre syndrome for which a urinary catheter can be inserted in order to provide immediate relief to the client.
options: Day 1: Urine output 1600 mL/24 hours
Day 2: Urine output 1200 mL/24 hours
Day 3: 200 mL/24 hours
Which one of the following interventions is indicated in this case?
Urinary catheter insertion
Administer a fluid bolus
Continue monitoring for another 24 hours
Order an ultrasound pelvis
Suprapubic catheter insertion

stem: A client with Guillain-barre syndrome needs to be intubated due to the high risk of involvement of his respiratory muscles. Counseling and consent for this procedure should primarily be done with which of the following?
rationale: While the client’s spouse and close relatives should be counseled regarding his condition the primary decision making should be done by the client himself as GBS does not cause any cognitive impairment. Category: Neurology, intensive care, multiple choice question
options: Spouse of the client
From the client himself
The primary doctor should make the decision
With the client’s parents
The ICU specialists should make the decision

stem: Out of the following nursing interventions which ones are needed for a client with Guillain-barre syndrome? Mark them on the list below.
rationale: Nursing interventions for clients with Guillain-barre syndrome consist of the following: Position changes every 2 hours and ROM exercises to prevent skin breakdown and muscle stiffness Anticipate and manage pain Provide compression boots and anti-embolism stockings to reduce the risk of DVT Monitor bowel habits as paralytic ileus may occur Monitor the respiratory rate and vital capacity as there might be paralysis of respiratory muscles Anticipate the need for communication strategies such as eye blinking or graphic cards as cranial nerve involvement may leave the client unable to vocalize Assess for mental health problems Category: Neurology, Rehabilitation, Multiple Response Question
options: Position changes every 2 hours
Passive ROM exercises
Edema massage
Compression boots
Monitor vital capacity
Monitor respiratory rate
Monitor ABGs
Anticipate the need for communication strategies
Keep the patient isolated to prevent risk of infections
Assess for anxiety and depression

stem: A 28-year-old female diagnosed with Multiple Sclerosis 5 years ago has come to the obstetric clinic for advice as she and her husband want to have a baby. Given below is her current medication list. Mark which medications should be discontinued prior to conception.
rationale: Copaxone is Glatiramer acetate which is an immunomodulatory agent contraindicated in pregnancy. Urecholine is bethanechol, a cholinergic medicine used for the treatment of urinary retention and also contraindicated in pregnancy. Category: Neurology, Pharmacology, Multiple response question Questions 99 to 102 are based on the case study given below Category: Neurology, immunology, rehabilitation, case study A 30-year-old female, diagnosed with multiple sclerosis 3 years ago, has been admitted with the complaint of difficulty walking, increasing fatigue, frequent falls, and blurring of vision. She had similar complaints on her previous admission. Her previous MRI had shown the presence of demyelination and plaque formation in the corpus callosum. The current MRI shows the presence of more extensive demyelination and plaque formation. Visual evoked response testing was performed which showed slow conduction in the optic nerves.
options: Copaxone
Lexapro
Centrum
Urecholine
Prilosec

stem: Out of the following differential diagnoses which one is most important to rule out in such a case?
rationale: The most important differential diagnosis to rule out in a case of multiple sclerosis is a cerebrovascular accident or a transient ischemic attack. Since the symptoms of multiple sclerosis and CVA/TIA mimic each other these differentials should be kept in mind.
options: Cerebrovascular accident
Guillain-barre syndrome
Transverse myelitis
Poliomyelitis
Neuropathy

stem: Which sub-type of multiple sclerosis does this client have?
rationale: The typical course of relapsing remitting multiple sclerosis is one where the client will experience symptoms of MS before going into complete remission. When there is a precipitating factor and sometimes without a factor, there be acute exacerbation of the disease which may last for weeks or months.
options: Progressive relapsing
Primary progressive
Secondary progressive
Relapsing remitting
Clinically isolated syndrome

stem: While caring for this client the registered nurse is going over how to assist her with unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP). On the list below highlight which precautions are to be taken when assisting this client with her ADLs.
rationale: The UAP should be taught not to fatigue the client while performing any physical activity as it can cause a relapse of symptoms. Activities should be stopped short of weariness. Walking aids with wide-based support or wheelchairs should be used. Schedule ADLs in the morning to minimize fatigue. Baths should be mild and not at extremes of temperature.
tokens: Use a walking aid with wide based support
Assist the patient to take hot baths
ADLs should be scheduled in the morning
Encourage the patient to complete a given physical activity even if they get tired
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: A client with multiple sclerosis is being discharged from the hospital and the nurse is giving her instructions regarding the management of symptoms and reducing the risk of exacerbations in the future. On the table below, mark which care components are necessary in this case.
rationale: See rationale.
rows: Wear long-sleeved shirts when going outside at dusk or in the early morning
Avoid extremes of heat e.g. saunas
Maintain moderate physical activity to reduce fatigue

stem: A 2-day-old male infant with a meningomyelocele has been admitted for surgical repair. The registered nurse will advise the mother to keep the baby in which one of the following positions while feeding.
rationale: Infants with spina bifida are always kept in the prone position pre-operatively, even while feeding, in order to prevent trauma to the sac. Category: Neurology, pre-operative care, pediatrics, multiple choice question
options: Supine
Semi-fowler’s
Side lying
Prone
Fowler’s

stem: An infant who had a meningomyelocele repair one day ago is being observed by the on-duty nurse in the NICU. She noticed that the infant has been getting lethargic over the past 6 hours and feeding less. On examination, his pupils are pointing downwards. While the nurse pages the physician, the infant has a tonic-clonic seizure which is immediately brought under control with anti-epileptic medication. Which one of the following complications of meningomyelocele repair has this client had?
rationale: Hydrocephalus and raised intracranial pressure are possible complications of a meningomyelocele repair. The presence of a sunset sign (pupils pointing downwards) indicates the development of hydrocephalus which in turn would lead to raised intracranial pressure. Seizures can be caused by raised ICP. Category: Neurology, post-operative care, pediatrics, multiple choice question
options: Epilepsy
Meningitis
Raised intracranial pressure
Encephalitis
Cerebrovascular accident

stem: A three-day-old female has been admitted to the neonatal ICU after meningomyelocele repair surgery. Which post-operative nursing support interventions are necessary for this client? Mark them in the list below.
rationale: Post-operative nursing care of an infant who has undergone a meningomyelocele repair consists of the following: Keep the infant in a prone or partial side-lying position A moist surgical dressing should be used Change the dressing 2 to 4 hourly Keep the surgical site clean of urine and feces Hips should be kept in slight abduction Perform passive range of motion exercises of all joints Use the crede maneuver to empty the bladder Category: Neurology, post-operative care, pediatrics, multiple choice question
options: Keep the infant in a prone or partial side-lying position
A dry surgical dressing should be used
Change the dressing 2 to 4 hourly
Keep the surgical site clean of urine and feces
Hips should be kept in slight adduction
Perform passive range of motion exercises of all joints
Use the crede maneuver to empty the bladder

stem: A 1-year-old male is being discharged after having a ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement. His surgical history shows that this is his third surgery due to complications with the shunt. Out of the advice listed below, which one is appropriate in this case?
rationale: Clients who have had multiple neurosurgical procedures are at increased risk of latex allergies and therefore the parents should be counseled to avoid using latex-containing products such as pacifiers and bottles. Category: Neurology, Pediatrics, post-operative care Questions 108 to 112 are based on the case study given below. Category: Neurology, autoimmune diseases, case study A 36-year-old female is admitted with the complaint of bilateral ptosis and weakness of both arms which progresses throughout the day. Her complaints have been increasing in severity for the past three weeks and she has been feeling more and more fatigued. She also complains of bilateral shoulder pain which worsens as the day progresses. Her medical history shows that she is 9 weeks pregnant. She works as a cleaner. On examination, power is ⅘ in both shoulders in all ranges of motions.
options: Restrict the baby's daily fluid intake to 1000 mL per day
Avoid using latex-containing products
Do not let the baby lie in a prone position
Measure head circumference daily
Do not use over-the-counter medications

stem: Out of the following diagnoses which one is most likely in this client?
rationale: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disease in which there are antibodies to the acetylcholine receptor. Clients will typically complain of fatigue in major muscle groups which increases throughout the day. There may be a precipitating factor for symptom exacerbation, such as pregnancy.
options: Guillain-barre syndrome
Transverse myelitis
Myasthenia gravis
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
Cerebrovascular accident

stem: On laboratory investigation, anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies are elevated confirming the diagnosis of myasthenia gravis.
rationale: A myasthenic crisis is an acute exacerbation of myasthenia gravis can be caused by various factors including pregnancy, stress, menstruation, viral illness, and immunization. During an acute exacerbation respiratory and swallowing muscle groups may be involved.
options: On the second day of admission, the client starts complaining of difficulty swallowing, choking on liquids, and difficulty breathing. She is at risk of __________________ as shown by ______________________.
Partial paralysis; difficulty breathing
A myasthenic crisis; involvement of swallowing and respiratory muscles
A deep vein thrombosis; the presence of anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies
A cerebrovascular accident; the presence of anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies
Developing heart failure; difficulty breathing

stem: On examination, the client’s respiratory rate is 30 bpm and her O2 saturation is 85%. The physician’s orders are given below. Highlight the interventions which the nurse must perform immediately.
rationale: Immediate actions would consist of interventions to reduce respiratory distress and decrease the risk of aspiration. For this the client would be administered supplemental O2, receive an anti-cholinesterase inhibitor and corticosteroids. The head of the bed should be elevated in order to prevent aspiration.
tokens: Provide supplemental O2 at 10 liters via facial mask
Elevate the head of the bed
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: After acute interventions the client’s condition stabilizes. On the table below mark which nursing interventions are indicated and contraindicated in this client.
rationale: In a client with myasthenia gravis the following nursing interventions are indicated: Provide a soft mechanical diet which is level 2 of the national dysphagia diet Thicken clear fluids Elevate the head of the bed to 90 degrees at meal times Activites should be paced throughout the day to preserve the client’s strength although more care activities should be done in the morning Airway patency, cough reflex, respiratory rate and effort should be monitored regularly Assess how much assistance the client requires for activities of daily living
rows: Provide level 3 of the national dysphagia diet
Elevate the head of the bed to 90 degrees when feeding the client
Activities should be performed all together in the morning

stem: After improvement in symptoms the client is being discharged. On the list below mark which self-care instructions would be appropriate for this client.
rationale: Self-care strategies for the client with myasthenia gravis are as follows: Record how well symptoms responded after inital few doses of Pyridostigmine Wear comfortable shoes with nonskid soles Do not perform any major activities before mealtimes Medications must be carried with the client at all times Wear a medical identification bracelet Sit upright when eating Sit in a chair with armrests to assist with getting up Do not discontinue medicine suddenly or without consulting a healthcare practitioner Eat food slowly and take a rest between bites
options: Record how well symptoms responded after inital few doses of Pyridostigmine
Wear comfortable shoes with nonskid soles
Complete any major activities before mealtimes
Medications must be carried with the client at all times
There is no need for a medical identification bracelet
Sit upright when eating
Sit in a chair with armrests
Discontinue medication once symptoms improve
Eat food slowly and take a rest between bites

stem: A 38-year-old female with myasthenia gravis was brought to the Emergency Department with sudden aggravation of symptoms. She is having difficulty breathing, choking on solids and liquids and her eyelid drooping has noticeably worsened since morning according to her husband. Her medical record shows that she has been taking medication for Hepatitis B, which was diagnosed three months ago.
rationale: Alpha-interferon is a drug used for the treatment of Hepatitis B infection and its use has been associated with myasthenia gravis symptoms. Therefore, out of the risk factors listed above this one is the most likely reason for aggravation of her symptoms. Other medications to be avoided include Botulinum toxin and D-penicillamine. Category: Neurology, autoimmune disorders, pharmacology, multiple choice question
options: Out of the factors listed below which one is most likely responsible for the acute exacerbation of myasthenia gravis symptoms?
Hepatitis B infection
Alpha-interferon
Entecavir
Telbivudine
Stress

stem: A 67-year-old male recently diagnosed with Parkinson’s Disease has been prescribed the following medication:
rationale: Dopamine antagonists such as Bromocriptine (Parlodel) may cause increased sleepiness and therefore clients must be cautioned to be careful while driving or to avoid driving. Amantadine (Symmetrel) may cause burning micturition. A levodopa/carbidopa combination (Sinemet) may lead to dry mouth and it should not be discontinued abruptly. It may also cause orthostatic hypotension. Category: Neurology, pharmacology, multiple response question
options: Tab Sinemet 25-100 TDS
Tab Parlodel ½ tab BD
Tab Symmetrel 200 mg OD
Out of the following precautions, which ones are necessary for this client? Mark them on the list below.
Do not drive for a few days until you have assessed whether or not you are experiencing increased drowsiness
Tab Parlodel does not cause nausea or vomiting
Tab Symmetrel may cause you to experience some burning while passing urine
Tab Sinemet may lead to increased salivation
If you experience headaches discontinue Tab Sinemet immediately
You may experience some dizziness when sitting and standing up

stem: A client with Parkinson’s Disease is being given self-care advice by the registered nurse during a scheduled clinic visit. On the table below mark which self-care instructions this client will need.
rationale: See rationale.
rows: Do not stop taking any medication without medical advice
Decrease fluid intake to reduce trips to the bathroom
Focus on gait while walking to stay upright

stem: A 36-year-old male, diagnosed with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis 4 years ago has been admitted for hospice care. After reviewing the disease progression over the past year, he has been advised to decide on ventilator support as he will most likely develop respiratory failure soon. After discussion with healthcare providers but against the wishes of his wife and parents, he has opted for being intubated and he does not want to be resuscitated. Out of the following interventions, which one is most appropriate in this case?
rationale: If a client’s cognition is not impaired they make the final end-of-life decision. Since cognition is not affected in ALS the client in this scenario has the right to make his own decision. Category: Neurology, Palliative care, Ethics, Multiple choice question Questions 117 to 121 are based on the case study provided below Category: Neurology, gastrointestinal system, case study A 38-year-old female with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis has been admitted due to complaints of difficulty swallowing liquids and frequent episodes of choking and coughing. She cannot walk due to the involvement of both lower extremities with disease progression. She is also unable to use her left arm although she can flex the fingers of the right hand.
options: The end-of-life decision for a client with ALS should be made by his next of kin
Follow the client’s end-of-life decision
The palliative care team holds the right to make the final decision for the client
The registered nurse should advise the client against signing a DNR
The next of kin should contact the client’s primary physician to help change the client’s decision

stem: The registered nurse is concerned that this client is at risk for _______________ due to _________________.
rationale: This client is especially at risk for developing decubitus ulcers due to the fact that she is unable to walk. Since she cannot use her upper extremities she will also not be able to perform any bed mobility thus increasing her risk of developing decubitus ulcers.
options: Pneumonia; coughing
Falls; lower limb paralysis
Decubitus ulcers; immobility
Aspiration; immobility
Upper respiratory tract infection; choking

stem: To prevent aspiration and improve nutritional status which nursing interventions out of the following are recommended for this client? Mark them on the table below.
rationale: Nursing interventions to prevent aspiration in a client with ALS consist of the following: Provide additional nutritional liquids Soft, mechanical diet is indicated Tuck the chin against the neck while swallowing to decrease the risk of aspiration Encourage the client not to talk at mealtimes Coordinate with a dietitian to make an adequate diet plan
rows: Lean head back while swallowing to decrease the risk of aspiration
Encourage the client to talk in between bites
The nurse should make an adequate diet plan

stem: The nursing notes include the following information:
rationale: Nursing interventions for the problems listed above consist of the following: Position changes every 30 minutes Increase fluid intake Administer laxatives to help pass stool although they are not recommended for chronic use. Mineral oil is contraindicated due to the risk of aspiration Schedule physiotherapist visits for ROM exercises Use a circulating mattress Initiate a bowel program
options: Client has not passed stool for 4 days
Urine output has decreased to 1000 mL per day
Presence of a grade 1 pressure ulcer over the sacrum
On the list below highlight which interventions are needed in this client.
Position changes every 30 minutes
Increase fluid intake
Administer laxatives and mineral oil to help pass stool
Schedule physiotherapist visits for ROM exercises
Use a firm foam mattress
Initiate a bowel program

stem: The nursing notes over three days show the following trend in the client’s symptoms:
rationale: Increased respiratory rate and respiratory effort indicate that the client is in respiratory distress. Shallow breaths, poor respiratory effort, and an impaired cough reflex all place the client at an increased risk of respiratory failure.
options: Based on the findings above the nurse will be most concerned about the client developing which one of the following complications?
Aspiration
Complete paralysis
Respiratory failure
Pneumothorax
Loss of consciousness

stem: Highlight, on the list below, which nursing interventions are indicated for this client now.
rationale: See rationale.
tokens: Suctioning of fluid in the mouth
Counsel the client and her family about the need for ventilator support
Advise the patient to get a tracheostomy
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: A primigravida mother has just delivered her baby who was diagnosed with anencephaly in utero. The couple was offered a medical abortion but refused due to religious reasons. Out of the following nursing interventions which ones are indicated for the parents and the child? Mark them on the list below.
rationale: Nursing intervention in the case of a baby born with anencephaly the parents should be given emotional support. Options for organ donation should be discussed and prevention of the condition in the subsequent pregnancy. A baby with anencephaly would not require ventilator support as the child would not survive even with ventilator support.
options: Provide emotional support to the parents
Clean the child and warm the child
Discuss options for organ donation
Transfer the child to the NICU for ventilator support
Discuss options for prevention of the condition in the next pregnancy

stem: The registered nurse in an assisted living facility is in charge of helping a 68-year-old female client with Alzheimer's to stay oriented and maximize independent living. Out of the following techniques which ones will be most effective for this client? Mark them on the table below.
rationale: Nursing care for a client with Alzheimer’s consists of the following: Help break down tasks into short, simple steps Keep the daily routine consistent Encourage socialization Use reality orientation techniques such as reminding the client about the day and date, discussing current events, labeling drawers and cabinets, etc. The client should be encouraged to perform ADLs as independently as safely possible Category: Neurology, Geriatrics, Grid question Questions 124 to 129 are based on the case study given below Category: Neurology, Emergency Medicine, Case study A 78-year-old male is admitted to the stroke unit after a left middle cerebral artery infarct which presented 3 hours ago as right-sided upper and lower limb weakness and left-sided facial paralysis. He is having difficulty speaking as his speech is slurred and he is responding out of context to the questions asked. He seems disoriented, irritable, and aggressive. On examination his vital signs are as follows: Pulse 110 bpm Respiratory rate: 20 bpm Blood pressure: 190/100 mmHg Temperature: 98.6 F O2 saturation: 98%
rows: Help break down daily tasks into short, simple steps
Keep the daily routine flexible to keep the client's short-term memory more active
Make use of reality orientation techniques
Perform maximum ADLs for the client

stem: Out of the following cues given in the scenario, which one requires the most urgent followup?
rationale: With a blood pressure of 190/100 mmHg the client would be unable to receive tissue plasminogen activator as a therapy for ischemic stroke. Furthermore, a systolic blood pressure greater than 180 mmHg along with signs of organ damage is a hypertensive emergency which must be dealt with immediately.
options: Right-sided upper and lower limb paralysis
Facial paralysis
Slurring of speech
Disorientation
Blood pressure: 190/100 mmHg

stem: The following orders have been given by the physician. Highlight which ones should be performed immediately by the nurse.
rationale: See rationale.
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: The bedside swallowing assessment shows that the client is is choking on liquids. He is also having difficulty swallowing solid foods and starts coughing. His blood pressure drops to 150/90 mmHg 20 minutes after receiving injection labetolol and he receives thrombolytic therapy. The nurse is concerned because the client is at risk for ________________ as shown by __________________.
rationale: A bedside swallowing assessment should always be performed for stroke patients as soon as possible since the presence of dysphagia places them at risk for aspiration pneumonia which would significantly increase morbidity and mortality after a stroke.
options: Reinfarction; blood pressure of 150/90 mmHg
Hemorrhagic stroke; blood pressure of 150/90 mmHg
Aspiration pneumonia; choking and swallowing difficulty
Dental caries; swallowing difficulty
Upper respiratory tract infection; choking on liquids

stem: Out of the following precautions which ones are indicated to prevent aspiration pneumonia in this client? Mark them on the list below.
rationale: Nursing interventions to prevent pneumonia consist of the following: Maintain oral hygiene Keep the client in an elevated position Check for food pocketing while eating Insert an NG tube only if there is severe swallowing impairment Repeat swallowing assessment at regular intervals Perform oral suctioning Oral fluids may be given cautiously
options: Maintain oral hygiene
Keep the client in a supine position
Check for food pocketing while eating
Insert an NG tube
Repeat swallowing assessment at regular intervals
Perform oral suctioning
Do not give any fluids except in IV form

stem: Out of the following nursing interventions, which ones are indicated for the care of this client? Mark them on the table below.
rationale: Nursing interventions for a left-sided (left side of brain involved, right side of body involved) stroke include the following: Perform range of motion exercises and provide compression stockings to prevent DVT Position changes every 2 hours Assist with ADLs Use short one or two word commands Use visual aids to help understand commands Explain to the family that comprehension is unimpaired
rows: Perform range of motion exercises of all joints
Decrease sensory stimuli in the room
Use short one or two word commands
Remind the client for performing daily activites
Explain to the family that comprehension is impaired

stem: The client is discharged one week later after his symptoms have improved and he is stable. Which self-care recommendations out of the list below will the registered nurse provide to this client? Mark them on the list below.
rationale: Self-care recommendations for this client will be as follows: Family members and caregivers should speak slowly and give the client sufficient time to reply Use a communication board Visit a physical therapist and occupation therapist to help re-learn ADLs Visit a speech therapist regularly When undressing remove clothes from the unaffected side first followed by the affected side Use assistive equipment while eating Encourage the client to feed themself as much as possible
options: Family members and caregivers should speak slowly and give the client sufficient time to reply
Do not use a communication board as it will hinder development of speech
Visit a physical therapist and occupation therapist to help re-learn ADLs
Visit a speech therapist regularly
When undressing remove clothes from the affected side, followed by the unaffected side
Use assistive equipment while eating
Encourage the caregiver to feed the client as much as possible

stem: A client with a right sided middle cerebral Infarction has been admitted to the stroke unit. When counseling the family members regarding which changes to expect in the client and how to care for them the registered nurse will include which information out of the list below. Mark them on the list.
rationale: The correct interventions are: The client will have behavior changes Music will not be helpful in providing information to a client with a right MCA Provide high-top tennis shoes for the client The client will be unable to recognize faces Combine auditory and visual methods of input Category: Neurology, geriatrics, multiple response question
options: The client will have behavior changes
Music may be used to help provide information
Provide high-top tennis shoes for the client
The client will be unable to recognize faces
Use print and visual methods of communication

stem: erebThe registered nurse is reviewing safety protocols to be followed if a client is having a seizure with the UAP. Which protocols out of the following are indicated in these clients? Mark them on the table below.
rationale: Safety protocols in a client having a seizure include: Lower the patient gently to the floor if out of bed Do not restrain the client Shift into a left lateral position Protect the head Loosen clothes Do not insert an airway if jaw is clenched Category: Neurology, emergency medicine, acute care, grid question
rows: Lower the patient gently to the floor if out of bed
Shift into a left lateral position
Tighten clothes to maintain joint integrity
Do not insert an airway if jaw is clenched

stem: A 33-year-old female, diagnosed with myasthenia gravis 6 years ago, is rushed to the emergency department by her husband with the complaint of sudden onset breathing difficulty. She has also been complaining of increasing difficulty in swallowing solids and liquids and is having difficulty forming sentences. The client is administered edrophonium which causes an increase in the respiratory rate and she starts drooling due to inability to swallow. Based on this scenario, the client is experiencing which one of the following?
rationale: In a cholinergic crisis a client with myasthenia gravis will complain of sudden onset respiratory distress, dysphagia and dysarthria. It can be distinguished from a myasthenic crisis by an edrophonium test which worsens symptoms in a cholinergic crisis. Category: Neurology, emergency medicine, multiple choice question Questions 133 to 135 are based on the case study given below Category: Neurology, trauma medicine, intensive care, case study A 28-year-old male admitted in the surgical ICU after a spinal cord injury due to a road traffic accident starts complaining of a sudden onset headache and feeling hot and sweaty. On examination, the client is flushed and sweating profusely. His vital signs show a pulse of 130 bpm and a blood pressure of 160/90 mmHg.
options: Myasthenic crisis
Cholinergic crisis
Cerebrovascular accident
Allergic reaction
Bell’s palsy

stem: Which one of the following complications is this client most likely experiencing?
rationale: Autonomic hyperreflexia occurs in clients with a spinal cord injury at or above the level of the T6 spinal segment. It occurs due to an exaggerated sympathetic response to a stimulus below the level of the injury. Clinically it manifests with increased blood pressure, bradycardia or tachycardia, flushing, excessive sweating and piloerection.
options: Cerebrovascular accident
Pulmonary embolism
Deep vein thrombosis
Autonomic hyperreflexia
Panic attack

stem: The physician orders for this client are given below. Highlight which ones the nurse must perform immediately in order to control the autonomic hyperreflexia for this client.
rationale: The most common cause of autonomic hyperreflexia in clients with sci is bowel or bladder distension. Therefore, after loosening the clothes and elevating the head of the bed it is essential to check for urinary retention and fecal impaction.
tokens: Elevate the head of the bed
Check for any cuts, bruises, lacerations or other injuries below the level of the lesion
Perform a fundoscopy to rule out raised ICP
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: Out of the parameters listed below which ones show that the client’s condition is improving, unchanged or worsening? Mark them on the table below. Questions 136 to 141 are based on the case study given below Category: Neurology, Trauma medicine, Acute care, Case Study
rationale: See rationale.

stem: A 24-year-old male is brought to the Emergency Department by his friends after a road traffic accident on his heavy bike which caused severe damage to the spinal cord. As soon as the client is in the ED which nursing interventions of the following are should be done immediately?
rationale: The immediate intervention required for a client with a spinal cord injury is to immobilize the spinal cord in order to prevent further damage. Airway patency should be assessed in case intubation is needed. Respiration should be assessed to identify if the client goes into respiratory failure.
options: Immobilize the spinal cord
Send the client for a CT scan of the brain to assess for head trauma
Confirm airway patency
Get a blood sample for ABGs
Assess consciousness level
Fundoscopy to check for signs of raised intracranial pressure
Monitor respiratory rate
Monitor depth of respiration

stem: On the first day of admission in the ICU the client starts complaining of dizziness and difficulty breathing. On examination, his vital signs are as follows:
rationale: Neurogenic shock occurs in clients with sci due to loss of the connections between the brain and the sympathetic nervous system. It manifests with bradycardia, increased respiratory rate and warm, flushed extremities.
options: Pulse: 55 bpm
Respiratory rate: 28 bpm
Blood pressure: 80/50 mmHg
Temperature: 97 F
O2 saturation: 78%
There is poor expansion of the chest wall and the client is restless and anxious. His hands are warm to the touch and he looks flushed. Which one of the following complications is he experiencing?
Cardiogenic shock
Spinal shock
Neurogenic shock
Autonomic hyperreflexia
Raised intracranial pressure

stem: The physician’s orders for this client are given below. Which ones must be performed for the client immediately to counteract the neurogenic shock? Highlight them on the list below.
rationale: The immediate intervention for neurogenic shock is to provide fluid resuscitation to counteract the effects of fluid pooling in the extremities. Phenylephrine may be given to cause peripheral vasoconstriction and atropine to raise the heart rate. An x-ray would be preferred over an MRI in the acute setting to confirm the presence of a vertebral fracture. O2 administration to keep the saturation normal.
tokens: Intravenous administration of a 500 mL bolus of normal saline
10 mg of phenylephrine in an infusion of dextrose saline
Range of motion exercises of all joints
X-ray of cervical and thoracic spine
MRI of cervical and thoracic spine
Oxygen administration at 100% via non-rebreather mask
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: A detailed neurological examination and an MRI of the spine shows that the level of the lesion is C7. A surgical fixation procedure is performed and a halo collar attached. Out of the following nursing interventions, which ones are indicated for this client? Mark them on the table below.
rationale: Nursing care in a client with a spinal cord injury consists of the following: Provide moist O2 supplementation and suctioning to remove secretions Mechanical or manual assistance for coughing The purpose of a halo collar is to keep the cervical spine immobilized while allowing the client to be mobilized Joint position changes every 2 hours Provide DVT prophylaxis with compression stockings, joint position changes every 2 hours, range of motion exercises at least 4 times a day and administration of anticoagulants. Chest physiotherapy Skin assessment should be performed at least twice daily Initiate a bowel and bladder care plan
rows: Counsel for a suprapubic catheter insertion
Make a bowel care schedule for the client

stem: At the time of discharge from the spinal cord rehabilitation center 3 months later the client has tetraplegia but elbow extension is present. He wants to know which tasks he will be able to perform independently. On the table below mark the tasks this client would be able to perform himself with the appropriate training.
rationale: See rationale.
rows: Driving a car modified with hand controls
columns: Can perform with an assistive device

stem: A 34-year-old male has been admitted to the spinal cord rehabilitation center after a road traffic accident in which he sustained damage to the T10 segment of the spinal cord. He does not have bowel and bladder continence and has had a urinary catheter inserted since the time of his accident. The registered nurse switches him to an intermittent catheterization schedule every 3 hours which is gradually prolonged to 5 hours. At the end of the first 5-hour interval, the urine volume passed after insertion of the catheter is 600 mL. What should be the next step?
rationale: In males, there should be an output of less than 500 mL when performing intermittent catheterization. If there is an output of more than 500 mL at any time, the time between catheterizations should be shortened. If there is consistently increased output the client should be switched back to an indwelling catheter. Category: Neurology, rehabilitation medicine, multiple choice question
options: A) Insert indwelling catheter
B) Maintain an intermittent catheterization schedule of 5 hours
C) Insert a suprapubic catheter
D) Shorten intermittent catheterization schedule to 3 hours
E) Send a urine sample for testing

stem: The registered nurse in the Neurology ward is teaching the components of bowel care to a client with SCI secondary to damage to the T5 spinal segment in order to allow him to integrate into the community more easily. Out of the list below, which statements would be beneficial when advising this client?
rationale: A bowel program in a client with an upper motor neuron injury will consist of the following: Follow a schedule for eating and bowel elimination Bowel elimination should be done 30 minutes after eating Start consuming 5 to 25 grams of fiber per day Administer a suppository after appropriate lubrication Apply abdominal pressure manually Remove feces by inserting the index finger into the rectum to digitally stimulate the defecation reflex Category: Neurology, rehabilitation medicine, multiple choice question
steps: Follow a schedule for eating and bowel elimination
Administer a suppository after appropriate lubrication
Apply abdominal pressure manually

stem: A 27-year-old male is admitted to the Neurology ICU after a road traffic accident which resulted in a complete spinal cord injury at the level of the T12 segment. On day 4 of admission the registered nurse notices bilateral edema in the lower limbs. The left lower limb is also erythematous and warm to the touch. The client does not complain of any pain and there is no tenderness on palpation. However, he is complaining of some dizziness when he is propped up and lower back pain when sitting up.
rationale: The index of suspicion for a thromboembolic event should be high in clients with a spinal cord injury. Since this client has a complete spinal cord injury he will not feel any pain or tenderness and the only signs of a DVT will be edema, redness, and warmth of the affected extremity. Category: Neurology, intensive care
options: The nurse suspects that the client is at risk of _________________ due to _________________.
Cellulitis; warmth and erythema in the lower limb
DVT; warmth and erythema in the lower limb
Pulmonary embolism; dizziness
Spinal shock; lower back pain
Neurogenic shock; dizziness and edematous, warm lower limb

stem: The registered nurse in the neurology clinic is counseling a female patient about migraine prevention. On the list below mark which substances should be avoided by this client.
rationale: Migraines can be triggered by foods high in MSG or salt (leading to fluid retention), nitrates, nitrites, alcohol, nicotine, and oral contraceptives. Category: Neurology, preventive medicine, multiple response question
options: Fava beans
Caffeine
Alcohol
Foods containing MSG
Drugs containing nitrates
Tobacco
Nicotine
Latex exposure
Oral contraceptives

stem: The registered nurse in the Neurology ICU is monitoring a 36-year-old male who has had recurrent seizures. Which precautions out of the following must be taken for this client? Mark them on the table below.
rationale: Nursing interventions to keep a client with seizures safe include the following: Remove all excess objects from the client’s surroundings Do not restrain the client Pad the side rails of the bed Loosen the clothing around his neck Do not keep anything in the client’s mouth Keep suctioning equipment handy Decrease stimulation around the client Category: Neurology, intensive care, grid question
rows: Remove all excess objects from the client’s surroundings
Restrain the client using padded cuffs and belts
Pad the side rails of the bed
Loosen the clothing around his neck
Keep a tongue depressor in his mouth to prevent him from biting his tongue

stem: When discharging a client with a seizure disorder which safety precautions must he be advised to take? Mark them on the list below.
rationale: Safety precautions given to a client with a seizure disorder include the following: Do not drive until you have been seizure free for at least 6 months Medication must be taken at the same time daily Do not go swimming alone Alcoholic beverages are not safe to consume Get as much physical activity and exercise as possible Decrease stress as much as possible Do not operate heavy machinery Wear a Medic Alert bracelet at all times Anticonvulsants cannot be taken with OTC medicines unless advised by a physician Category: Neurology, preventive medicine, multiple response question
steps: Medication must be taken at the same time daily
Do not go swimming alone
Decrease stress as much as possible
Do not operate heavy machinery
Wear a Medic Alert bracelet at all times

stem: A 25-year-old female is brought to the Emergency Department after severe head trauma in a road traffic accident which has led to a basilar skull fracture as identified through a CT scan. The registered nurse while caring for her notices that there is a clear discharge from both nostrils. Which one of the following tests is essential in this case?
rationale: In the setting of a skull fracture it is essential to test any discharge from the skull for glucose as CSF tests positive for glucose. The presence of CSF leakage indicates a dural tear. Category: Neurology, emergency medicine, multiple choice question
options: Skull x-ray
Test CSF for glucose
Spinal tap
Fundoscopy
IgE levels

stem: A 2-year-old female is brought to the pediatrics emergency department with the complaint of a sore throat, nasal stuffiness and a temperature of 103 F documented at home. According to the mother the child’s temperature has risen rapidly from 100 F to 103 F from morning to afternoon. The nurse will be most concerned that the child will be at risk for ________________ as a result of __________________.
rationale: The most immediate complication that this client is at risk for are febrile seizures which can occur in children between the ages of 3 months and 3 years. There will be a sudden onset tonic-clonic seizure and a rapid rise in temperature above 101 F puts children at greater risk. Category: Neurology, pediatrics, emergency medicine, prioritize hypothesis question Questions 149 to 151 are based on the case study given below Category: Neurology, Pediatrics, Case study A 1-year-old male has been admitted to the Pediatric ward after he was brought to the Emergency Department due to a single episode of a tonic-clonic seizure. His mother has given a history of an upper respiratory tract infection with a fever of 102 F for the past 2 days. Family history reveals that the client’s older brother had a febrile seizure at the same age. There is also a family history of asthma and allergies.
options: Epilepsy; rise in body temperature
Respiratory distress; nasal stuffiness
Rheumatic fever; sore throat
Febrile seizure; rapid rise in body temperature
Meningitis; upper respiratory tract infection

stem: Which factors in the case scenario above support a diagnosis of febrile seizure in this client? Mark them on the list below.
rationale: Febrile seizures are seen in children between the ages of 3 months and 3 years and are more likely to occur in boys. A family history of febrile seizures increases the risk. Febrile seizures generally occur at temperatures above 101 F.
options: 1-year-old
Male gender
Upper respiratory tract infection
Fever of 102 F
Family history of febrile seizures
Family history of asthma
Family history of allergies

stem: Which nursing interventions are indicated in this client? Mark them on the table below.
rationale: Nursing interventions for febrile seizures include the following: Administer acetaminophen 4 hourly for fever (Aspirin is contraindicated in children due to the risk of Reye’s syndrome) Keep the child in a cool room Remove extra layers of clothing Give warm, tepid baths Encourage cool liquid intake
rows: Keep the child in a cool room
Give cold baths to reduce body temperature

stem: After the appropriate workup and treatment the client is discharged. Out of the following list which statements are appropriate for this client when advising the parents. Highlight them on the list below.
rationale: Nursing advice given to the parents of a child who has had a febrile seizure consists of the following: There will not be any permanent neurological damage as a result of the febrile seizure The most accurate temperature reading will be taken via a thermometer placed in the axilla for one minute After giving anti-pyretics use cold sponging to reduce fever in case of future infections Cold sponging should be done on the forehead and in the folds of the groin and axilla The child is at increased risk of febrile seizures in the future Questions 152 to 156 are based on the case study given below Category: Neurology, emergency medicine, geriatrics, case study A 66-year-old male is brought to the Emergency Department after a motor vehicle accident. There was no direct head trauma but he became unconscious after the crash. He is currently complaining of a headache. On examination, he is conscious and oriented but seems a little drowsy.
tokens: There might be some permanent neurological damage as a result of the febrile seizure
The most accurate temperature reading will be taken via a thermometer placed in the axilla for one minute
After giving anti-pyretics use cold sponging to reduce fever in case of future infections
Cold sponging should be done only on the forehead
The child is at increased risk of febrile seizures in the future
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: What is the most immediate intervention required in this client?
rationale: The most likely diagnosis in this scenario is a subdural hematoma based on the client's age and mode of injury as well as the presence of drowsiness. If a subdural hematoma is suspected the most immediate intervention is to determine the patency of the airway and check respiration in case intubation is needed.
options: Monitor blood pressure and pulse
Check arterial blood gases
Get an emergency CT scan done
Assess airway and monitor respiratory rate
Perform a skull x-ray

stem: The client is diagnosed with a subdural hematoma based on CT scan findings. Out of the following nursing interventions, which ones are indicated for this client? Mark them on the table below.
rationale: Nursing interventions in a client with a subdural hematoma consist of the following: Assess GCS every 2 hours and compare from baseline Perform pupillary assessment Monitor vital signs 2 hourly Check nose for blood or CSF Monitor ABGs Maintain the client in a side-lying position to preserve the patency of the airway Monitor for signs of raised ICP
rows: Assess GCS every 2 hours and compare from baseline
Check nose for blood or CSF
Maintain the client in a supine position

stem: The client’s nursing notes show the following information on the second day of admission.
rationale: Raised intracranial pressure can occur after a subdural hematoma and is indicated by a rise in the systolic blood pressure which leads to a wide pulse pressure as well as a drop in the respiratory rate.
options: Based on the trend given above the nurse will be concerned that the client is at risk for _______________________.
Neurogenic shock
Raised intracranial pressure
Pontine infarct
Normal pressure hydrocephalus
Pulmonary edema

stem: Which immediate nursing interventions are indicated in this client? Highlight them in the list below.
rationale: See rationale.
tokens: Raise the head of the bed
Insert Foley catheter for input-output charting
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: Which parameters out of the following will let the nurse know whether the client’s condition is improving, unchanged or worsening. Mark them on the table below.
rationale: See rationale.
rows: Right pupil 4 mm and non reactive

stem: A 33-year-old male has come to the Neurology clinic for his follow up visit. He has been taking medication for seizure control for the past 3 months and he has noticed that his urine has been a brownish color since starting his new prescription. Which one of the following medicines is most likely causing this symptom?
rationale: The side effects of phenytoin include ataxia, gingival hyperplasia and urine discoloration. Category: Neurology, pharmacology, multiple choice question
options: Carbamezepine
Levodopa
Lameotrigine
Gabapentin
Phenytoin

stem: A 78-year-old female who was recently diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease is brought to the Neurology clinic by her son who is complaining that there has been no improvement in her symptoms. Her medical record shows that she has been prescribed Tab Sinemet two weeks ago. Out of the following actions which ones should be undertaken by the registered nurse? Highlight them on the list below.
rationale: Medications given for Parkinson’s disease take a few months to be effective and their effect is mostly palliative. That is, they will slow down disease progression but will not cure the client completely. Physical and occupational therapy is recommended to help clients with their ADLs. Category: Neurology, pharmacology, geriatrics, multiple response question
tokens: Make an appointment for the client with the neurologist for a change in medication
Reassure the client and her son that the medicine takes a few months to be effective
Recommend physical and occupational therapy sessions to help the client cope with her symptoms
Add Amantadine to the client’s prescription
Explain that the effects of Parkinson’s medicines are palliative
Explain that symptoms will resolve completely after a minimum period of 1 year
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: A 2-year-old boy has been admitted with the diagnosis of a neuroblastoma over the left adrenal gland. Out of the following components of physical examination, which one should not be performed in this client?
rationale: Abdominal palpation is contraindicated in clients who have an adrenal tumor to avoid a hemorrhagic event in the tumor mass. Category: Neurology, endocrinology, multiple choice question
options: Abdominal palpation
Abdominal percussion
Daily weight checks
Genital examination
Neurological examination

stem: A 16-months-old male with a neuroblastoma of the right adrenal gland is being discharged from the pediatric oncology ward. He is to undergo 4 further sessions of radiotherapy. On the table below mark which client care advice will be provided to the parents in this scenario.
rationale: Client care in this case would include the following: Do not wash off the radiations markers Keep a daily weight record Take a high-calorie, high-protein diet with increased fluid intake Bring the child to the hospital immediately in case of any nausea and vomiting after therapy Do not discontinue medications suddenly Category: Neurology, endocrinology, pediatrics, grid question
rows: Wash off the radiation markers after discharge
Restrict water intake to 1 liter per day
Bring the child to the hospital immediately in case of any nausea and vomiting after therapy

stem: A 16-year-old female presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset weakness of the right side of her face. She is having difficulty closing her right eyelid, swallowing and chewing from the right side. She gives a history of having fever and flu symptoms for the past 3 days. Out of the list below, highlight the nursing interventions which will be indicated for this client.
rationale: This scenario shows a case of Bell’s palsy in which the following nursing interventions are indicated: Explain to the client that the paralysis is temporary Provide artificial tears for the right eye Keep the right eye taped at night Stay upright at mealtimes Eat foods with a soft consistency Perform facial muscle exercises Category: Neurology, emergency medicine, multiple response question
tokens: Explain to the client that the paralysis might be permanent
Provide artificial tears for the right eye
Keep the right eye taped at night
Insert an NG tube for feeding
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: A 23-year-old male has been admitted in the neurology ICU due to meningitis leading to seizures and cerebral edema. After fulfilling the medication orders the registered nurse is monitoring him for complications and response to medication. Out of the following parameters which one is the most important indicator of the response to intravenous mannitol therapy?
rationale: Cerebral edema can lead to a raise in intracranial pressure. Intravenous mannitol would help in fluid diuresis and thus lead to a decrease in the intracranial pressure, making it the most important parameter to be monitored in clients with meningitis and cerebral edema. Category: Neurology, pharmacology, intensive care medicine, multiple choice question
options: Increased systolic blood pressure
Increased urine output
Decreased heart rate
Decreased intracranial pressure
Improved GCS score

stem: A 33-year-old female with a history of tonic-clonic seizures has been on combination therapy with Phenytoin and Carbamezapine for the past 1 year and she has been seizure free for the past 7 months. She has come for her regular followup and wants to know when she will be able to discontinue the medicines and what precautions she should take. On the list below highlight the medication advice which the registered nurse will give her.
rationale: The following precautions are indicated when taking medicines for seizures: Medications may be continued indefinitely but withdrawal can be attempted after 3 years of being seizure free Once 3 years have passed medications can be stopped gradually Take medicines with food to decrease gastrointestinal side effects Monitor hepatic enzyme levels regularly A therapeutic level of 10-20 mcg/L shoul be maintained for Phenytoin Oral contraceptives should be taken with caution Category: Neurology, Pharmacology, Multiple response question
tokens: Medications will be discontinued after 3 years of being seizure free
Once 3 years have passed medications can be stopped immediately
Do not take medicines with food
A therapeutic level of 10-20 mcg/L shoul be maintained for Phenytoin
Oral contraceptives should be taken with caution
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: The registered nurse in the Neurology clinic is providing a fact sheet to a client with Parkinson’s disease related to her medication. Since the client has been prescribed Sinemet which foods should she avoid eating with her medicine? Mark them on the list below
rationale: Foods with a high Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) content should not be eaten with dopaminergic drugs. This includes veal, lamb, pork, egg yolks and potatoes. Category: Neurology, Pharmacology, Multiple response question Questions 166 to 168 are based on the case study given below. Category: Neurology, ophthalmology, pharmacology, case study A 76-year-old male with Parkinson’s disease is brought to the Emergency Department by his neighbor due to severe redness and pain in the right eye along with headache, nausea and vomiting for the past hour. The client is unable to give a proper history due to disorientation but his medical record shows that he was prescribed some new medications 3 days ago. He also has a history of asthma. An eye examination confirms the presence of acute narrow angle glaucoma.
options: Fava beans
Veal
Chicken
Eggs
Potatoes
Lentils
Blueberries
Yogurt

stem: Out of the drugs listed below, which one is most likely the precipitating factor for this client’s symptoms?
rationale: Carbidopa-levidopa is contraindicated in narrow angle glaucoma as it can precipitate acute angle closure leading to a sudden rise in intraocular pressure.
options: Amantadine
Carbidopa-levodopa
Bromocriptine
Donepezil
Benztropine

stem: Which nursing interventions should be undertaken immediately for this client?
rationale: The immediate nursing goal in acute angle closure glaucoma is to decrease the intra-ocular pressure. The client should be asked to lie on their back as this moves the lens back and encourages drainage of fluid from the eye. Intravenous acetazolamide 500 mg is given immediately. The beta-blocker of choice is betaxolol in glaucoma and timolol is contraindicated if there is a respiratory disorder. Monitor the intraocular pressure at hourly intervals. Pilocarpine should be administered after one hour of acetazolamide and beta blocker therapy. Once the intraocular pressure has been stabilized the client should be referred to an ophthalmologist.
options: Keep the patient upright at 90 degrees
Administer intravenous Acetazolamide 500 mg immediately
Administer timolol
Monitor the client’s intra-ocular pressure at hourly intervals
Instill 4% pilocarpine eye drops immediately
Refer to an ophthalmologist once IOP decreases

stem: Once the client's intraocular pressure has returned to normal and an opthalmology consult obtained he is discharged. Out of the self-care instructions listed below, which ones would be relevant for this client? Mark them on the table below.
rationale: Self-care instructions to the client consist of the following points: The prescribed eye drops should be taken regularly for the rest of the client's life to prevent blindness Always carry eye drops when travelling Avoid any heavy lifting as straining would cause an increase in the intraocular pressure Prevent constipation Avoid driving at night Remove small objects from the frequently walked areas of the house
rows: The prescribed eye drops should be taken regularly for at least 1 year
Always carry eye drops when travelling
It is safe to drive at night
Remove small objects from the frequently walked areas of the house

stem: A 45-year-old male visited his family physician for a regular check up. On checking his family history
rationale: In clients with a family history of glaucoma eye checkups for intraocular pressure should be done every 2 years after the age of 40 years. Category: Opthalmology, preventive medicine, multiple choice question Questions 170 to 172 are based on the case study given below Category: Ophthalmology, pharmacology, peri-operative care, case study
options: the registered nurse noticed that his father and grandfather had had a history of glaucoma. Out of
the following statements which one is the most suitable advice for this client?
A) Follow up with the ophthalmologist for IOP checks every 5 years
B) Use eye lubricant drops daily
C) Always sleep supine on your back to decrease IOP
D) Follow up for IOP checks every 2 years
E) Avoid driving at night

stem: A 68-year-old female with cataracts has been admitted for a cataract removal procedure. Out of the following medications, which one will be instilled in her eye to cause mydriasis prior to the procedure?
rationale: Phenylephrine is a mydriatic agent given prior to cataract surgery to dilate the pupil via its alpha adrenergic action.
options: Pilocarpine
Phenylephrine
Carbachol
Saline drops
Phenylephrine

stem: 3 days after the surgery the client presents with persistent pain in the operated eye along with excessive lacrimation, redness, headache, nausea and vomiting. There is no purulent discharge or itching. The registered nurse is concerned that the client is suffering from ________________________ which is a possible complication of cataract surgery.
rationale: Raised intraocular pressure is a possible complication of cataract surgery in which the client will present with consistent pain as well as redness and watering of the eye and symptoms such as headache, nausea and vomiting.
options: Retinal detachment
Conjunctivitis
Raised intraocular pressure
Incomplete removal of cataract
Retinal vein embolism

stem: Once the client has been treated for the raised intraocular pressure and stabilized she is discharged. On the table below, mark which components of post-operative care the registered nurse will recommend to this client.
rationale: Client teaching for self-care consists of the following: Wear an eye shield at night for 2 to 3 weeks Do not use any over-the-counter medications Do not sleep on the affected side Avoid heavy lifting Avoid strenuous exercise immediately after the procedure There is no need to avoid bending over Wear dark glasses when going outside
rows: Wear an eye shield at night for 2 to 3 weeks
Use an over the counter anti-allergy eye drop if there is any itching
Do not sleep on the affected side
It is to safe to continue strenuous exercise

stem: The registered nurse in the ophthalmology clinic is teaching a client how to administer eye drops properly. Out of the following statements, which one is appropriate?
rationale: Eye drops should be administered to the middle third of the lower eyelid after pulling the eyelid down to create a conjunctival sac. Category: Ophthalmology, pharmacology, multiple choice question
options: Apply eye drops to the inner third of the lower eyelid
Apply eye drops to the middle third of the lower eyelid
Apply eye drops to the outer third of the lower eyelid
Apply eye drops to the center of the sclera
Do not blink for one minute after applying eye drops

stem: A 57-year-old female is receiving a regular checkup as required by her job. On slit lamp examination there are subretinal hyaline deposits although she does not report any significant visual loss. Out of the following actions, which ones are most appropriate in this case? Mark them on the list below.
rationale: The presence of subretinal hyaline deposits (also called Drusen) indicate that the client is at increased risk for macular degeneration. Zinc and antioxidant supplements should be prescribed and the client should monitor any vision changes using an Amsler grid. Category: Ophthalmology, preventive medicine, multiple response question
options: Call the client for a follow-up visit after 6 months
Prescribe zinc supplementation
Prescribe Vitamin E supplementation
Prescribe Vitamin D supplementation
Use an Amsler grid to check for any visual changes
Do not drive at night
Avoid alcohol use

stem: What should be the positioning of a client after retinal reattachment surgery?
rationale: After a retinal reattachment surgery clients should be positioned in the prone position in order to maintain the retinal gas bubble. Category: Ophthalmology, post-operative care, multiple choice question
options: Supine position
Prone position
Semi-fowler’s position
Right side lying
Left side lying

stem: A 56-year-old female has presented to the Ophthalmology clinic with the complaint of itching, redness, and watery discharge from the left eye for the past 3 days. There is no concurrent headache, nausea, or vomiting. She has a history of glaucoma for which she administers Latanoprost drops daily. After examination, her intraocular pressure is found to be normal, and based on the findings a diagnosis of viral conjunctivitis is made. Out of the following self-care advice, which ones are indicated for this client? Mark them in the table below.
rationale: Nursing self-care advice: Use cold compresses to relieve itching Do not stop using drops for Glaucoma, use separate bottles for the infected and uninfected eye and label them clearly Wear sunglasses when going outside to help with photosensitivity Keep towels and washcloths separate from those of other people Always wash the uninfected eye first and then the infected eye when cleansing face Do not rub eyes Refrigerate eye drops to help with itching Category: Ophthalmology; grid question
rows: Use cold compresses to relieve itching
Stop using Latanoprost drops for one week to avoid transferring infection from the infected to the uninfected eye
Keep towels and washcloths separate from those of other people
Always wash the infected eye first and then the uninfected eye when cleansing face
Rub eyes gently if there is any itching
Refrigerate eye drops to help with itching

stem: The registered nurse in the ENT clinic is teaching a group of nursing students the correct way to irrigate the ear canal to clean out wax. Out of the following methods, which one is the most accurate way to irrigate the canal?
rationale: The stream of fluid should be directed up and back towards the posterior wall of the ear canal so that the fluid will get behind the wax and other detritus and wash it out. Never aim directly at the eardrum to avoid the risk of perforation. Category: ENT, outpatient care, multiple choice question
options: Aim the stream of water towards the eardrum to clean out the entire ear canal
Direct the stream of fluid towards the anterior wall of the canal
Aim the stream of fluid towards the inferior portion of the ear canal
The stream of fluid should be directed towards the superior portion of the canal
Aim the fluid towards the back of the posterior wall of the ear canal

stem: A 22-months-old girl who was admitted with a diagnosis of meningitis is being discharged after successful treatment of her infection and symptoms. While giving her parents follow-up instructions, which one of the following tests will the registered nurse classify as absolutely necessary?
rationale: Meningitis is among the most common causes of severe bilateral deafness in children and therefore all clients with meningitis should have a hearing test as soon as they have recovered. Category: Neurology, ENT, infectious diseases, multiple choice question
options: Intraocular pressure
Hearing test
Swallowing assessment
Electrocardiogram
Kidney function test

stem: A 27-year-old male with recurrent ear infections has presented to the ENT clinic. A previous ear examination showed the presence of thick, yellowish pus as well as a perforation of the tympanic membrane. A sample of pus discharge from his ear was sent for culture and sensitivity, showing susceptibility to the following drugs. On the list below, mark which drugs would be contraindicated in this client.
rationale: Gentamycin is an aminoglycoside that is ototoxic if exposed to the middle ear and can lead to deafness if used in a client with a perforated tympanic membrane. Neomycin sulfate and cortisporin may lead to severe contact dermatitis in clients with a perforated tympanic membrane. Category: ENT, pharmacology, multiple response question
options: Ciprofloxacin
Gentamycin
Neomycin sulfate
Cortisporin
Ofloxacin

stem: A mother has brought her 2-year-old daughter to the Pediatric clinic with the complaint that she is inattentive, does not follow instructions, and does not speak properly apart from a few words. She was delivered via vaginal delivery at 38 weeks of gestation and there were no complications during the ante-natal, perinatal, or postnatal periods. She has achieved all of her motor milestones on time and does not display any aggressive or antisocial behavior. The mother is frustrated with her daughter’s lack of attention and wants a psychological evaluation of the child. During the examination, the client displays an interest in toys placed before her and indicates what she wants. The registered nurse, based on the above information, will suggest which one of the following tests?
rationale: Based on the scenario given above the client is most likely suffering from hearing loss. This manifests in children as speech delay, inattention, and failure to follow instructions. In a 2-year-old with a speech delay, a hearing test is essential before making a diagnosis of a psychological disorder. Category: ENT, pediatrics, multiple choice question
options: Slit lamp examination
Electroencephalogram
Hearing assessment
Cranial nerve X examination
IQ test

stem: A 26-year-old male is being discharged after undergoing a myringoplasty secondary to otitis media which was treated successfully with antibiotics. Out of the self-care instructions below which ones are indicated for this client? Mark them in the table below. Self-care instructions after a myringoplasty consist of the following: Avoid bending over for at least 3 weeks Avoid driving for one week Avoid air travel for a time period between 1 to 4 weeks Avoid drinking liquids with a straw Sneeze with an open mouth Avoid swimming for at least 4 weeks Category: ENT, post-op care, grid question
rationale: See rationale.
rows: Avoid bending over for at least 3 months
Avoid air travel for at least 2 weeks
Avoid swimming for at least 4 weeks
Sleep in a prone position for at least 2 weeks

stem: A 27-year-old female has visited the ENT clinic with the complaint of a progressive decrease in hearing over the past 2 years. She is concerned because she has had to ask people to speak louder and louder in order to hear them. Her husband has noticed, however, that she seems to hear better in a noisy background. She also complains of on and off tinnitus in the left ear. There is no history of vertigo, loss of consciousness, nausea, or vomiting. Out of the following conditions, which one is this client most likely suffering from?
rationale: Hearing loss which improves in a noisy background is the hallmark of otosclerosis, a condition caused by an overgrowth of spongy bone around the stapes footplate and the oval window. This leads to progressive hearing loss. Category: ENT, multiple choice question Questions 183 to 187 are based on the case study given below Category: Musculoskeletal system, emergency medicine, case study
options: Meniere’s Disease
BPPV
Otosclerosis
Otitis media
Tympanic membrane rupture

stem: A 29-year-old male was brought to the Emergency Department after a road traffic accident involving a bike collision. He has sustained severe lacerations on the left leg and after examination and x-ray, a diagnosis of a left tibial fracture is made. Out of the following nursing interventions, which one should be done immediately?
rationale: In the case of a fracture the affected limb should be immediately immobilized to prevent neurovascular damage from the movement of the bone shards. Pain management is also an immediate concern.
options: Perform plaster of paris casting
Refer the client for a 3D CT scan to assess the fracture
Monitor vital signs
Assess airway
Immobilize the affected limb

stem: The client undergoes an open fixation and internal reduction procedure for the fracture immediately and is shifted to the surgical ward. On the table below mark which nursing interventions are indicated for this client.
rationale: See rationale.
rows: Icing of the lower limb for pain management

stem: The nursing notes over the next 24 hours show the following information:
rationale: This scenario shows a case of compartment syndrome which is diagnosed based on clinical findings. Given that the anterior compartment of the leg is the most common site of an acute compartment syndrome and the client has signs and symptoms of neurovascular compromise the most likely diagnosis is acute compartment syndrome.
options: The registered nurse is concerned that the client is developing a _____________________ due to the presence of ____________________.
Deep vein thrombosis; neurovascular compromise
Compartment syndrome; neurovascular compromise
Cellulitis; pain and color change
Pulmonary embolism; cyanosis and pain
Malalignment of fracture; increasing pain

stem: A diagnosis of compartment syndrome is made after measuring the pressure within the anterior compartment of the leg which gives a reading of 36 mmHg. On the list below, highlight which actions should be undertaken by the nurse immediately.
rationale: See rationale.
tokens: Keep the left lower limb below the level of the heart
Take consent from the client for a fasciotomy
Get a repeat x-ray of the fracture site
Reassess on the visual analog scale for pain
instruction: Highlight the findings that require follow-up.

stem: A fasciotomy is performed for the client and he is shifted to the surgical ICU for monitoring. Out of the parameters given below which ones will show whether his condition is improving, unchanged, or worsening? Mark them in the table below.
rationale: See rationale.
